Science Supports Reducing Children's Sodium Intakes.
science clear... kids more sodium than what safe recommended.
Schools play important role helping kids stay healthy.
part effort bring sodium moderate levels kids, U.S. Department Agriculture (USDA) established sodium limits school meals, which being carried three gradual phases until 2022-2023 school year.
only health issues associated with much sodium problems like high blood pressure heart disease problems adults, kids.
Science strongly supports link between less sodium intake healthier lives even children teens.
example, kids high sodium diets about more likely have elevated blood pressure than kids lower sodium diets.
And, rate high blood pressure increasing American children.
There been noise about some sodium studies, leading confusion helping efforts undermine nutrition standards school meals.
large body scientific research indicates that lowering sodium intake lowers blood pressure adults children.
impossible schools meet USDA's sodium limits.
Actually, more than percent schools successfully meeting updated meal standards.
Some schools already meeting USDA's 2017 targets.
Many companies already offer foods that meet target limits USDA.
variety methods exist that help reduce sodium foods modelling suggests that some newly developed ingredients could make impact.

This post updated January include links AMD's Apple's statements.
major security flaw surfaced that's thought affect Intel microprocessors since least 2011, some processors and, less processors.
Unusually, exploits, called Meltdown Spectre, take advantage processors' hardware rather than software flaw, they circumvent security schemes built into major operating systems.
Makers those operating systems racing roll Meltdown, which easier exploit pursue, according Register, which first reported effort Tuesday.
operating system redo will almost certainly lead performance reductions some systems during certain uses.
fact that there's largely security researchers Graz University Technology, Austria, were unaware vulnerability until last month.
Daniel Gruss Graz colleagues specialize side-channel attacks, ways exploit systems using data gleaned from physical implementation system rather than software flaw.
2016 they examined ways harden core operating system kernel against such attacks, came with scheme they called KAISER.
KAISER prevents computer processes user applications from managing kernel memory spaces which might, instance, give someone access your log-in information cryptographic you'd like keep safe.
does strictly separating kernel memory spaces processor cache.
That might sound simple, peculiarities architecture, which most server processors based, make nontrivial task.
They published paper July 2017.
some point they stumbled across posting Anders Fogh.
attempted read protected kernel data using quirk modern processors keep busy while waiting slow compute processes their data.
such situations processors perform speculative execution.
That they start working what they expect should next task, discarding result they guessed wrong.
Fogh couldn't attack work, Gruss's colleagues Michael Schwarz Moritz Lipp did.
related attack, which they call Spectre, potentially wider reaching because "breaks isolation between different applications.
allows attacker trick error-free programs, which follow best practices, into leaking their secrets," according website.
Unfortunately, KAISER general Spectre, which thankfully trickier pull than Meltdown.
There's been some concern about whether KAISER will slow computers down much.
Gruss colleagues tested Intel Skylake processor less than percent performance loss.
However, they've seen bigger numbers older processors, performance varies depending what processor doing.
example, program that needs deal with large amounts small files will likely slowdown because interface with kernel frequently, says Gruss.
Emails seeking comment from were returned press time.
since posted statement saying that architectural differences make processors immune Spectre.
(AMD refers more official names "branch target injection" "bounds check bypass.")
says patches will keep products safe from Meltdown.
Google, which discovered issue independently, listed what's vulnerable, what's not, things blog post.
says some high-end Cortex processors vulnerable, that Cortex-M products heavily used low-power systems not.
full list some technical details here.
